coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensor based on quaternized carbon dots and Ellman’s test for ultrasensitive detection of dichlorvos
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juying
Tian, Zhengbin
Xie, Huizhi
Tian, Qingyuan
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: Here we first reported an ultrasensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) biosensor of dichlorvos based on carbon dots and the traditionally colorimetric Ellman’s test. In Ellman’s test, the absorption intensity of 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid anion (TNB−) at 412nm can be used for the determination of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and its inhibitor. We chose TNB− as energy acceptors to construct a FRET sensing system of AChE and its inhibitor. Quaternized carbon dots (Q-CDs) were chosen as energy donors to form an effective FRET system. By a two-step synthetic method, the Q-CDs with high quantum yield (34.2%) were successfully prepared. Experimental results showed TNB− can quench the fluorescence of Q-CDs to a large extent, which indicated an effective FRET between Q-CDs and TNB− was successfully established. As the inhibitor of AChE and common organophosphorus insecticide, dichlorvos can be detected using this sensing system. Under optimal conditions, the linear range of dichlorvos was 5.0×10−11–1.0×10−7 M. Owing to the high sensitivity and green fluorescent material with high quantum yield, this FRET sensing system was adaptable for automated analyzers using in the rapid determination of a great quantity of samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Organophosphorus compounds
Acetylcholinesterase
Ellman’s test

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DDVP in phosphate buffer
Measurement and calibration of DDVP in DDVP-spiked juice samples
Formation mechanism and characterization of Q-CDs
The quenching effect of TNB− on the fluorescence of Q-CDs
The anti-interference ability, stability and reproducibility
Determination of DDVP in DDVP-spiked juice samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375079, 51402175) and Project of Development of Science and Technology of Shandong Province, China (No. 2013GZX20109).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of measuring principle of OPs sensor.

(a) Illustration of formation of Q-CDs; TEM micrographs at different magnifications (b) 200nm (with the corresponding particle size distribution histogram, inset) and (c) 50nm (with the HRTEM micrograph, inset).
PL emission spectra of Q-CDs at 380nm excitation (with the photo of Q-CDs under UV light at 365nm, inset) and the UV-vis absorption spectrum of TNB−.

(a) PL spectra of the Q-CDs/AChE/ATChCl/DTNB solution over time, (b) the plot of τ0/τ versus the concentrations of ATChCl, effect of (c) pH and (d) ionic strengths (ionic strengths were controlled by various concentrations of NaCl) on the quenching efficiency.

(a) PL spectra of the Q-CDs/AChE/ATChCl/DTNB solution incubated with different concentrations of DDVP; (b) calibration plot for detecting DDVP. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensor based on quaternized carbon dots and Ellman’s test for ultrasensitive detection of dichlorvos

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we first reported an ultrasensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) biosensor of dichlorvos based on carbon dots and the traditionally colorimetric Ellman’s test. In Ellman’s test, the absorption intensity of 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid anion (TNB−) at 412nm can be used for the determination of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and its inhibitor. We chose TNB− as energy acceptors to construct a FRET sensing system of AChE and its inhibitor. Quaternized carbon dots (Q-CDs) were chosen as energy donors to form an effective FRET system. By a two-step synthetic method, the Q-CDs with high quantum yield (34.2%) were successfully prepared. Experimental results showed TNB− can quench the fluorescence of Q-CDs to a large extent, which indicated an effective FRET between Q-CDs and TNB− was successfully established. As the inhibitor of AChE and common organophosphorus insecticide, dichlorvos can be detected using this sensing system. Under optimal conditions, the linear range of dichlorvos was 5.0×10−11–1.0×10−7 M. Owing to the high sensitivity and green fluorescent material with high quantum yield, this FRET sensing system was adaptable for automated analyzers using in the rapid determination of a great quantity of samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the relatively low persistence and high effectiveness for insect, organophosphorus compounds (OPs) are the most extensively used pesticides in modern agriculture. OPs can raise serious human health and environmental concerns due to their powerful inhibition effect on the activity of cholinesterase. According to the statistics, about two million people die from OPs poisoning every year. Therefore, the detection of OPs has drawn great attention regarding to the concerns of the public safety and environmental protection. Conventional analytical techniques, such as liquid chromatography, gas chromatography and tandem mass spectrometer [1–3], possess good selectivity, high sensitivity, and can distinguish different pesticides. However, they are relying upon expensive equipments, skilled personnel and time-consuming treatment of sample, which leads to these approaches impractical for regular environment and food monitoring. Several fluorescent detection methods of OPs [4–9] with high sensitivity, low-cost, and operational simplicity and rapidity have been developed in the past decades. However, the fluorescence materials of these methods suffered from some drawbacks, such as tedious preparation steps, poisonous synthetic conditions, and toxicity of material etc. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have shown their appealing advantages [10–13], such as good water-solubility, high chemical inertness, good photostability, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Up to date, the sensing systems based on CDs were mainly used for the detections of metal ions, such as Hg2+ [14], Cu2+ [15] and Fe3+ [16]. In previous work, we have constructed one kind of sensitive OPs sensor based on L-Tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots and tyrosinase [17].

In this paper, we designed a novel fluorescent biosensor for the determination of OPs. The sensing principle included catalysis reaction of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) (Eq. (1)) and Ellman’s test (Eq. (2)).
(1)
a c e t y l t h i o c h o l i n e +
H
2
O
→
A C h E
thiocholine + acetic acid
(2) 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoicacid)(DTNB)+thiols→5-thio-2-nitrobenzoicacid(TNB, yellow)+disulphide

The Ellman’s test was the most widely used colorimetric technique for the detection of thiol compounds [18,19]. In the Ellman’s test, 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) reacted with molecules containing a free thiol group producing highly yellow-coloured 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid (TNB) (Eq. (2)), which could be detected at 412nm by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. AChE can catalyze acetylthiocholine to form thiocholine (TCh) (Eq. (1)), so this method can be used for the determination of AChE and its inhibitor [20]. This method was simple, accurate and economic, and suitable for automated analyzers [21]. Owing to high sensitivity of fluorescence technology, we tried to establish FRET sensor using TNB− as energy acceptors (quencher) based on the high extinction coefficient of TNB−. In consideration of electrostatic interaction, quaternized carbon dots (Q-CDs) were chosen as energy donors. One kind of Q-CDs with a quantum yield of 34.2% was sucessfully prepared by surface quaternization of polyethyleneimine modified carbon dots (PEI-CDs). An effective energy transfer system was formed between the Q-CDs and TNB−. As compared with the FRET system between PEI-CDs and TNB−, the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency of the proposed system was improved by about 35.6%. This FRET sensing system can be used to detect the activity of AChE and its inhibitor. As an example of organophosphorus pesticides, dichlorvos was detected. Experimental results showed that the enzyme inhibition rate was proportional to the logarithm of the dichlorvos concentration in the range 5.0×10−11–1.0×10−7 M with the detection limit (S/N=3) of 1.9×10−11 M. This determination method showed a low detection limit, good selectivity and high reproducibility. Furthermore, owing to the high quantum yield of Q-CDs and the strong absorption of TNB−, this sensing system had good anti-interference capability, potential applications in real samples for OPs, and was suitable for automated analyzers for the rapid determination of large numbers of samples.

Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Basf Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Branched polyethyleneimine (M.W. 10,000) was purchased from Chengdu Aike Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), acetylcholinesterase (EC 3.1.1.7, from electrophorus electricus, lyophilized powder), 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid), acetylthiocholine chloride (ATChCl) and dichlorvos (DDVP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and betaine were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The redistilled water used throughout all the experiments. The fruit juice samples were obtained from a local supermarket.

Citric acid (0.30g), branched polyethyleneimine (0.80g) and 20mL redistilled water were mixed, and then added into a 40mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 7h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to remove the large dots. Then, the solution was extensively dialyzed against redistilled water in a dialysis membrane (15000 MW CO) for 3d, the obtained carbon dots were named as PEI-CDs.

0.020g betaine, 0.020g EDC, 0.020g NHS and 20mL PBS 8.0 were mixed and reacted at 25°C for 6h. Then 20mL PEI-CDs solution was added into the above solution and reacted at 25°C for 4h. The resulting yellow solution was extensively dialyzed against redistilled water in a dialysis membrane (15000 MW CO) for 3d, the obtained carbon dots were named as Q-CDs. By freeze-dried method, yellow powder was obtained for further characterization. The powder was dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 2mg/mL for further use.

The morphology and microstructures of Q-CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, USA) operated at 200kV. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (USA, VARIAN). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected using a UV-2450 Shimadzu Vis-spectrometer (Japan). Zeta potential was performed using a zetasizer analyzer (ZS 90, Malvern). Fluorescence lifetime was measured by an F900 time resolved spectroscope (Edinburgh).

200μL of Q-CDs (2mg/mL), 300μL of AChE (1.0U/mL), 20μL of ATChCl (150mM), and 200μL of DTNB (50mM) were mixed in a series of 10mL volumetric flasks, and then, the mixture was diluted to the mark with 0.2M PBS and reacted at 35°C. The fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded under excitation at 380nm over time.

200μL of Q-CDs (2mg/mL), 300μL of AChE (1.0U/mL), 20μL different concentrations of ATChCl, and 200μL of DTNB (50mM) were mixed in a series of 10mL volumetric flasks, and then, the mixture was diluted to the mark with 0.2M PBS (pH=8.0) and reacted at 35°C for 1h. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 465nm respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DDVP in phosphate buffer
ce:section.ce:para:
The procedure for DDVP determination was as follows: (1) 200μL of Q-CDs (2mg/mL), 300μL of AChE (1.0U/mL), 100μL DDVP and 200μL of DTNB (50mM) were mixed in a series of 10mL volumetric flasks, and then, the mixture was diluted to the mark with 0.2M PBS and incubated at room temperature; (2) 20μL ATChCl (150mM) was added into the above solution and reacted at different temperature, the fluorescence signal was monitored over time. The inhibition rate of DDVP (I%) was adopted as a signal for the detection of DDVP. I% was calculated using Eq. (3),
(3)
I %
=
F
D D V P
−
F
A C h E
F
0
−
F
A C h e
× 100 %
where F0 was the initial fluorescence intensity without ATChCl, FAChE stood for the fluorescence intensity after quenching of TNB−, FDDVP was the fluorescence intensity after the DDVP inhibition reaction and TNB− quenching experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and calibration of DDVP in DDVP-spiked juice samples
ce:section.ce:para: Different quantities of DDVP were added into the fruit juice samples, and then the mixed solution was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane, and mixed with an equal volume of 0.2M PBS (pH=8.0) to adjust the pH of the solution to 8.0. DDVP determination procedure was as follows: (1) 40μL of Q-CDs (2mg/mL), 60μL of AChE (1.0U/mL) and 40μL of DTNB (50mM) were added into 1860μL sample solution and incubated at 35°C for 10min; (2) 4μL of ATChCl (150mM) were added into the above solution, after 7min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 380nm. The results were compared with the calibration curve, and the DDVP concentration was obtained.

In previous reported fluorescent sensors of OPs based on AChE [5,6], the detection principles were according to the quenching effect of H2O2, which was the product of the AChE/acetylcholine/choline oxidase system. This paper constructed one kind of novel fluorescence sensing system of OPs. Fig. 1 illustrated the fluorescence switch mechanism for the detection of OPs based on Q-CDs and Ellman’s test. The sensor was based on the following principles: (1) AChE can catalyze acetylthiocholine to form thiocholine; (2) thiocholine can react with 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) to form 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid, which can quench the fluorescence of carbon dots by fluorescence resonance energy transfer; (3) OPs can inhibit the activity of AChE and recovery the fluorescence of carbon dots. In this FRET sensing system, TNB− was employed as the fluorescence acceptors (quencher) owing to the high extinction coefficient around 412nm. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of TNB− was overlapped with the common fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. Q-CDs were chosen as the energy donors owing to following reasons: (1) In near-neutral solution, the main existence form of TNB was TNB− anion; (2) Q-CDs with plentiful positive charges were easy to form an effective energy transfer system with TNB− via electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism and characterization of Q-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hu et al. have reported polyethyleneimine modified carbon dots with a high quantum yield [22]. To obtain Q-CDs with a high quantum yield, the Q-CDs were prepared by surface quaternization of PEI-CDs. PEI-CDs were obtained by simple hydrothermal reaction using citric acid as carbon resource and PEI as the surface passivation reagent. By the conjugation reactions between amino groups on the surface of PEI-CDs and carboxyl groups of betaine, the Q-CDs were prepared. The zeta potential of CDs increased from +22.8mV to +37.2mV by modification with quaternary ammonium salt. These results indicated that the surface of Q-CDs contained more positive charge than PEI-CDs, and Q-CDs were easier to react with NTB− via electrostatic interaction than PEI-CDs. The following of fluorescence quenching experiments supported this viewpoint. The morphology of Q-CDs was characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 2 b and c). The Q-CDs were well-dispersed spherical dots with average diameters of 9.2nm (based on statistical analysis of more than 200 dots). The inset of Fig. 2b showed these nanodots were mainly distributed in the range of 4–14nm. The HRTEM image of Q-CDs in the inset of Fig. 2c showed the lattice spacing of 0.31nm, which may be attributable to the (002) facet of graphite [23].

Fig. S1a showed photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the Q-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 380nm in the following experiments owing to the strongest fluorescence emission. Fig. 3 showed that the fluorescence emission was overlapped the absorption of TNB− at large extent. The insert of Fig. 3 showed that the Q-CDs solution exhibited a bright blue color under a UV lamp (365nm). The quantum yield of carbon dots was calculated to be 34.2% at 380nm excitation using quinine sulfate as standards, which was much higher than that of previous reported quaternized carbon dots (4%) [24]. The effects of the pH and ionic strength of solutions and UV exposure on the fluorescence intensity of Q-CDs were studied (Fig. S1b–d). From Fig. S1b, we can see that the fluorescence intensity of Q-CDs can be negligible in the pH range from 5.0 to 10.0. This phenomenon may be attributed to the quaternary ammonium group on the surface of Q-CDs, the existence form of which made no obvious change in pH 5.0–10.0. Fig. S1c showed that there was no obvious change in PL intensity at different ionic strengths. Fig. S1d indicated that photo bleaching effect of UV on the Q-CDs can be negligible. The good stability of Q-CDs was in favor of the determination of DDVP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of TNB− on the fluorescence of Q-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 a showed a typical time-dependent fluorescence intensity of Q-CDs solution in 0.2M PBS buffer (pH=8) containing 0.03U/mL AChE, 0.3mM ATChCl and 1mM DTNB. From Fig. 4a, we can see that the PL intensity of the system gradually decreased with reaction time up to 10min, revealing that an effective energy transfer system was formed between Q-CDs and TNB−. To further confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime experiments were carried out (Fig. 4b). After mixing with 0.03U/mL AChE, different concentrations of ATChCl and 1mM DTNB, the decay time of mixture system decreased with the increase of ATChCl concentrations (Fig. S2). Fig. 4b exhibited a linear relationship between τ0/τ and the different concentrations of ATChCl in the range of 2.0×10−6–1.0×10−5 M. These results indicated the change of the fluorescence lifetimes with the concentrations fit with the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (4)) and the fluorescence quenching was a dynamic quenching process [25].
(4)
τ
0
τ
= 1 +
k
s v
C
A T C h C l
where τ0 was the fluorescence lifetime without ATChCl, τ stood for the fluorescence lifetime after quenching of TNB−, ksv was the quenching coefficient. Carboxylic acid functionalized carbon dots were chosen as the control experiment, the quenching effect of TNB− on Q-CDs can be ignored. These phenomena indicated that the inner filter effect can be excluded. Experimental results showed the quenching efficiency of this system at 10min was up to 72.94%. As compared with the energy transfer system between PEI-CDs and TNB−, the quenching efficiency of this system was increased by 35.6%. Fig. 4c showed the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching (I0 and I stood for the PL intensity of Q-CDs before and after quenching experiment, respectively). In the pH 8.0 PBS, the quenching efficiency was the highest. This phenomenon was owing to following reasons: (1) In pH 8–9 solution, the activity of AChE was higher, so more TNB− came into being; (2) The extinction coefficients of TNB− in PBS 8.0 solution may be greater [18]. From Fig. 4d, we can see that the influence of salting strength on the quenching efficiency can be ignored.

As a kind of widely used organphosphorus pesticides, dichlorvos was detected using this sensing system. To obtain high sensitivity, critical experiment conditions have been optimized, such as the concentration of enzyme, the concentration of substrate, the reaction temperature and the incubation time of AChE with DDVP. In optimization test, the concentration of DDVP was fixed at 1.0×10−9 M. The inhibition rate of DDVP (I%) was adopted as a signal for the detection of DDVP. DDVP can be hydrolyzed in basic solution. The optimum pH for the activity of AChE was 8.0–9.0. The optimum pH for quenching efficiency of TNB− was 8.0. In comprehensive consideration of the stability of DDVP, the quenching efficiency of TNB− and the activity of AChE, pH was fixed at 8.0 in the following experiments. The incubation time of AChE with DDVP was fixed at 10min by initial experiments. From Fig. S3, we can see that the inhibition rate was increased and then decreased along with the reaction time. These results were owing to following reasons: when the reaction of the system without DDVP was most complete, the reaction of the system with DDVP still took place, which led to the decrease of the inhibition rate. Fig. S3 showed that the optimal experimental conditions as follows: (1) the concentrations of AChE and ATChCl were 0.03U/mL and 0.3mM, respectively; (2) the temperature of reaction was 35°C; (3) the reaction time was 7min.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, different concentrations of DDVP were detected. When the concentration of DDVP increased in the range of 5.0×10−11 M–5.0×10−7 M, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b exhibited a good linear relationship between the inhibition rate and logCDDVP in the range of 5.0×10−11–1.0×10−7 M. The regression equation was I (%)=292.97+28.10 logCDDVP (R2 =0.995). The detection limit for DDVP at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was estimated to be 1.9×10−11 M (4.2×10−6 ppm), which was much lower than the maximum residue limit as reported in the European Union pesticides database as well as that from the U.S. Department Agriculture (0.01ppm). Under the same conditions, malathion and paraoxon were detected (Fig. S4). The regression equation of malathion was I (%)=306.7+30.36 logCmalathion (R2 =0.995) in the range of 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−7 M, and the regression equation of paraoxon was I (%)=346.3+31.07 logCparaoxon (R2 =0.997) in the range of 1.0×10−11–1.0×10−8 M. The detection limits for malathion and paraoxon were estimated to be 4.8×10−11 M and 3.5×10−12 M. These results indicated that different kinds of OPs can be detected using this sensing system. Table S1 showed the comparison of the different fluorescent sensing systems of OPs, suggesting the sensitivity of our sensing system was higher than that of previously reported fluorescent sensing systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-interference ability, stability and reproducibility
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed method for DDVP detection was further evaluated with various coexistence substances added. Fig. S5 showed the interference effect of inorganic ions, sugar, and organic acids on the determination of DDVP. As shown in Fig. S5, the results suggested that 106-fold for NaCl and KCl, 400-fold for CaCl2 and Zn(Ac)2, 200-fold for FeCl3, CuSO4 and ascorbic acid, 104-fold for glucose, sodium acetate and sodium citrate, 200-fold for glycerol had no influence on the signals of 1.0×10−9 M DDVP with deviations below 5%. These results showed that there was little interference from commonly existed substances. Thus, the new sensitive method displayed high selectivity for the determination of DDVP.

The stability and reproducibility of this sensor system were evaluated by determination of 1.0×10−9 M DDVP. The sensor retained 99.2% of its initial response after the Q-CDs was kept in refrigerator at 4°C for 30days, which indicated that the sensor had good stability. The reproducibility was investigated by five parallel prepared Q-CDs. The relative standard deviation was 6.3%, which suggested that the sensor displayed good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DDVP in DDVP-spiked juice samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the accuracy, repeatability and selectivity of the proposed method, different concentrations of DDVP in DDVP-spiked juice samples were determined and the results were listed in Table S2. From Table S2, it can be seen that the recoveries of the samples were found to be in the range 99–103%, and the RSDs were less than 6.0%. The results revealed that it was an applicable method for organophosphate compound-contaminated food samples.

In conclusion, we have first constructed one kind of fluorescence resonance energy transfer system between Q-CDs and TNB− for the determination of DDVP. By modification of PEI-CDs with quaternary ammonium salt, the Q-CDs with a quantum yield of 34.2% were obtained. Because the Q-CDs possessed more positive charges than the PEI-CDs, they can form more effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer system with TNB−. Under the same conditions, the quenching efficiency of TNB− on carbon dots increased by 35.6% after quaternization of PEI-CDs. The detection limit for DDVP using this sensing system was close to pM. This method was expected to use in automated analyzers for the rapid detection of large numbers of samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375079, 51402175) and Project of Development of Science and Technology of Shandong Province, China (No. 2013GZX20109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.03.092.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methods of sample preparation for determination of pesticide residues in food matrices by chromatography–mass spectrometry-based techniques: a review
Screening and quantification of pesticides in water using a dual-function graphitized carbon black disk
Microwave accelerated selective soxhlet extraction for the determination of organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides in ginseng with gas chromatography/mass spectrometry
Luminescence recognition of different organophosphorus pesticides by the luminescent Eu(III)–pyridine-2, 6-dicarboxylic acid probe
Optical detection of organophosphorus compounds based on Mn-doped ZnSe d-dot enzymatic catalytic sensor
A simple and sensitive fluorescence biosensor for detection of organophosphorus pesticides using H2O2-sensitive quantum dots/bi-enzyme
Selective fluorescence turn-on and ratiometric detection of organophosphate using dual-emitting Mn-doped ZnS nanocrystal probe
Upconversion nanoparticle-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay for organophosphorus pesticides
Photoluminescent sensor for acetylcholinesterase inhibitor determination
Amphiphilic egg-derived carbon dots: rapid plasma fabrication, pyrolysis process, and multicolor printing patterns
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
A fluorescent turn-off/on method for detection of Cu2+ and oxalate using carbon dots as fluorescent probes in aqueous solution
Graphitic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Determination of biothiols by a novel on-line HPLC-DTNB assay with post-column detection
A new and rapid colorimetric determination of acetylcholinesterase activity
History and new developments of assays for cholinesterase activity and inhibition
Multifunctional carbon dots with high quantum yield for imaging and gene delivery
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Juying Hou is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. She received her master degree in physical chemistry from Fuzhou University in 2005. She received her doctor degree in Food Science from Shandong Agricultural University in 2015. Her current research interests include preparation and application of nano functional material, new method for environmental pollutant analysis.

Zhengbin Tian received his bachelor’s degree in material chemistry from College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University in 2013. He is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University and his research interest is preparation and application of nano functional material.

Huizhi Xie received her bachelor’s degree in material chemistry from College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of technology in 2015. She is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University and her research interest is preparation and application of nano functional material.
Qingyuan Tian is a college senior of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University in applied chemistry.

Shiyun Ai is a professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. He received his master degree (physics chemistry) and doctor degree (analytical chemistry) from Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University in 1995 and in 2004, respectively. His current research interests include chemically modified electrode, bioelectroanalysis, preparation and application of nano functional material, new method for environmental pollutant analysis.
